item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) After some testing they discovered it can be used as evacuation
2)You want to be sure your evacuation method survive some damage.

Or they just plain up wanted to know about the material.
If they would destroy the anomalous pool, they would still have extremely durable super-plastic to study and possibly reverse engineer.
Applications of extremely durable plastic would be valuable in all sorts of fields

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's mostly because old Foundation was more into strange experiments.
Also, maybe, they had a good reason to believe that it could resist damage. Or damage to the pool wouldn't damage the portal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why did they attempt to destroy it? Their job is to contain anomalies, not to test how durable they are. If it turned out that this pool isn't drill-proof then that would possibly be an SCP gone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jesus this thing is horrifying for a few reasons. First, the missing kids are never brought up again. Second, only 37.8kg of material can be brought through at a time. That is heavier than any modern space suit, and about 4 times heavier than a suit with its life support system. Not only would the Foundation have to engineer the lightest space suit to date, but after all of that, they could only bring in a handful of materials one person at a time. Then, to send one more person with a few kg of measly materials, 6 D-class would have to be killed off. The sheer number of people that would have to die for this moon base makes the transcontinental railroad look like a lego playset in comparison. Jesus. Very dated in that Series 1 kind of way, but this one has aged better than most, and the horror is unique.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
IIRC, Thaumiel is not just "scip what is useful to foundation" (as there are plenty of those) but "scip what is explicitly used to contain other scips".

Pretty much this, but there's also a certain level control and understanding inherent in Thaumiel. That is, they still don't trust Thaumiel scips, but they're generally things that the Foundation reverse engineered or modified themselves, or something they feel like they could fix if it broke.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
IIRC, Thaumiel is not just "scip what is useful to foundation" (as there are plenty of those) but "scip what is explicitly used to contain other scips".

Then again, that's not a catch-all definition and I can think of at least two exceptions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If the Foundation is using/going to use it as a means of evacuation, could it technically be Thaumiel?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this SCP for a lot of reasons.

This is such a critical SCP for the Foundation's survival and it is, in fact, a child's plastic pool. That alone makes this concept amazing.

As I read I was never unaware of exactly what this SCP was, so when the term "dialed" was used and it was later explained it was horrifying. The cold, calculated decision to end these D-class personnel's lives in exchange for a moon base was sobering; the fact that the means by which this was achieved was a children's pool gave the perfect balance to it.

This is such a gem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"I said I wanted a Moon Base, and I don't care how many people I have to throw through it, I will get a Moon Base!"

+1 to this comment alone

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They weigh too much to take through 120, and even if they didn't it'd just prolong the suffering, getting back would be hideously expensive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why not deploy some of those inflatable Bigelow habitats at the Langrage points? It wouldn't be THAT expensive and would prevent pointless loss of life.

item.title: Re: Re-rate?
item.content:encoded: It's not really super-esoteric mind-blowing though.

item.title: Re: Re-rate?
item.content:encoded:
This was written /long/ before Thaumiel existed.

Keep in mind, series 1 was chronologically the earliest written skips.

item.title: Re-rate?
item.content:encoded: I may not know that much, but I'm just putting this out here- considering this is used for the containment and transport of SCPs, wouldn't this be considered Thaumiel?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why the hell did they try to destroy it so many times? It did nothing but be useful, and even though they got lucky and it was indestructible, it was extremely likely to just… Break. And now you've lost your magic space portal. And why did they keep trying to destroy it so many times?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Object Classes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No page exists there.

item.title: Location 1
item.content:encoded: The real-life USS Nassau was decommissioned in 2011. Suggest either blacking out the name or changing it to another ship.

item.title: Re: edit
item.content:encoded: Got it, let me know if I missed any and I'll go back and fix those too.

item.title: Re: edit
item.content:encoded: Thanks for letting staff know; Roget fixed it. :D

item.title: edit
item.content:encoded:
when i was reading this article, i noticed things such as

"an scp ship"
and,
"an scp base'

these should really be changed to

"a foundation ship

"a foundation base"

or something

item.title: Re: Deserved Touch-up...
item.content:encoded: Hurleyman77, instead of replying to your own comments, edit your previous post using the "edit" function under the "options" tab to the lower right of every comment. That prevents spam buildup, and it's in the rules. :)

item.title: Re: Deserved Touch-up...
item.content:encoded: …and: redacting containment procedures makes them difficult to follow, and naming specific individuals makes things difficult when they are killed/transferred/promoted/unavailable. It'd be easy to say "temporary command of the acting Security and Research facility heads (currently Captain __ and Dr __, respectively)." if having the redacted names was really important.

item.title: Deserved Touch-up...
item.content:encoded: I really like this one, which is why I think it ought to have a touch up. The technical/sterile feel gets lost pretty often in phrases like the ones Litfried and Dr Reach mentioned as well as my pet peeve "[adjective] in [attribute]," in this case "pastel pink in colouration," et. al. Standardizing the descriptions of the color of each destination (just 'place/color') would also help it feel more like an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Uuuuh I've been meaning to mention this…

It is unresponsive to manipulation by organic or inorganic means, but the substance ripples and shimmers systematically and regularly, suggesting it exists physically on another dimension.

lolwhy? This feels like a massive non sequitur. I mean, why of course it exists into another dimension, it ripples and shimmers systematically and regularly! It's the most scientific hypothesis possible!

Not that I don't like it, it's just that this specific sentence makes no sense. Upvoted nonetheless for the very same reasons good @Drewbear mentioned earlier.

I can almost hear an O5 saying "I said I wanted a Moon Base, and I don't care how many people I have to throw through it, I will get a Moon Base!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's cool and it's classic, but by modern standards, the tone is atrocious ('amazing tensile strength', 'vast expenditure of money and D-class', 'through the usual channels', etc) which is a but of a shame. I kind of think it would benefit from a bit of a re-touch, but also as it's classic, it should perhaps remain as it is.
The pool hits a well-deserved +100. Huzzah and jubilation!

item.title: Re: SCP Review
item.content:encoded: Eh. It's at +96. I wouldn't have done it, but whatever.

item.title: Re: SCP Review
item.content:encoded: Because I thought more people should read it. Not all calls for review are "Kill it! Kill it with fire!"

item.title: Re: SCP Review
item.content:encoded: Wait, why was this brought up for review?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally I like it, and it's mentioned in loads of tales and occasionally other articles. More people should see this one.

item.title: SCP Review
item.content:encoded:
Read, review, rate, O My Childrewn

This really is one of my favorite SCPs on the site. I utterly love space stuff, and the idea that the Foundation has a fully functional Lunar base is one I adore. The fact that they created it by intentional mass-murder on an epic scale makes it even better, because, to me, it illustrates how the Foundation is totally heartless. Someone decided that creating a private moonbase was worth the extermination of thousands, maybe even TENS of thousands of lives, and decided that was an acceptable tradeoff.

Essentially: "Yay, we have a moonbase! We only had to kill the entire population of New York City to do it!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They really should make a little probe thing to deorbit those corpses at the lagrange points. Someone's going to notice them eventually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love that there's nothing about the missing children. That's really where the article hammers in the horror, thinking about these kids. I also agree we should add that picture from above in, looks wonderful for the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thirded.

I know some pages have multiple images appear on them, randomised when the page is generated. Could be interesting if this image could be made to generate the five possible colours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Greenland looses about 267 billion tonnes of ice annually, what if someone used 120 in, say 50 years and dropped to their death? Wouldn`t that be fun?

[[html]]
http://www.agu.org/news/press/jhighlight_archives/2008/2008-11-11.html
[[/html]]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconded!

item.title: Re: Location 1: Pacific Ocean
item.content:encoded:
Just so we're clear, you know that forum posts are supposed to be out of character, right? This is right on the line, maybe a little over.

Also, the ship's public identity is redacted.

item.title: Location 1: Pacific Ocean
item.content:encoded: The SCP Ship in position of Location 1 is currently decommissioned. I recommend blacking out the name of the ship's public identity as displaying it would also be a great security oversight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It gets my vote for what it's worth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's pretty damn neat. Ought to get approval to add that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the process of learning CGI, I made an image for this SCP because it was the simplest thing I could think of.
http://www.pelpix.info/scp120-5.png

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Me too, I love the moon base. This SCP is so neat, but every time I read it I think about how brutal it is to the D-class. "The Himalayas aren't necessarily deadly? Well too bad, we're killing you anyway." I understand why they'd have to do it with no one there to retrieve them, but I still feel bad. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the idea of a hidden moon base and the mental image of this cloud of bodies floating around at the Lagrange points tickles me for some reason. A great source of biologicals if/when we get around to building space stations there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Looks like we've forgotten about this one…

I'm for changing it to Safe (despite my previous statement to the contrary). I'd third MrUnimport's seconding, but I'm still a mere peon, and my opinion doesn't carry such weight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No harm done, yoric.

Seconding change to Safe-class. Multi-class SCPs had better have a good reason for it, and I can't see any reason here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aldaris, you're completely and totally wrong. Read this for insight on what the security classes actually mean, and do try not to spout off opinions as fact in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd imagine it's because, while we don't understand why or how it works, the end to the means is predictable. In other words, it does the same thing every time it's used. In my opinion, the Safe/Euclid classification is justified.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Euclid means it does something weird that pysics as we know it cannot and will not explain, how does teleporting you to the moon NOT qualify it for Euclid?

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: Well, that can always be waived in emergencies.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: I suppose this is the sort of thing that might be used by O5, and only safe objects can do that…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I made it Safe instead of Safe/Euclid. I can't see anything Euclid about it at all (if you leave it alone, nothing happens).

[Edit:] Reverted to Safe/Euclid before the Mass Edit. Suggest reclassify as Safe when the time comes.

item.title: Re: Coordinates?
item.content:encoded: Agreed

item.title: Coordinates?
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't the latitudes and longitudes be blacked out or something? It seems like a massive security oversight to me, given that these are emergency evac points and all.
